## How to Start a Book Club

Running an AI Literacy in Education conversation series is, at its core, a lot like running a book club with a few caveats: your participants are busy, your topic is fast-moving, and you can't count on anyone to have the time to do the reading. That last constraint, it turns out, is a rather helpful feature. It pushed me to design conversations that were genuinely accessible, and the discussions were richer for it.

What follows is a practical overview of what went into organizing the series, offered as a starting 
point for anyone hoping to run something similar at their own institution.

### Reading and Preparing the Materials

The bulk of the behind-the-scenes work was reading and curating the relevant materials myself. Rather than assigning readings to participants, I came to each session having done that work on their behalf, so I could surface the ideas that mattered most and frame them in ways that were immediately discussable.

### Structuring the Conversation Questions

The most important design decision was writing questions that allowed participants to engage 
meaningfully without prior reading. Faculty and students are busy, and asking them to read in advance is often asking them not to come. Instead, I built questions around lived experience, opinion, and reflection: things everyone in the room already had access to. This kept the barrier to entry low while still allowing the conversation to go deep, with a wider range of experiences in the room.

### Logistics: Space, Posters, and Publicity

The practical side of things was straightforward but time-consuming: booking meeting spaces, 
designing and printing posters, and getting the word out to students and faculty. A well-run conversation series lives and dies by whether people know it's happening and feel genuinely welcomed into the space.
